LITERATURE CARD
Pathophysiology of Diabetic Retinopathy: The Old and the New

1. Bibliographic Metadata
Full citation (APA 7): Kusuhara, S., Fukushima, Y., Ogura, S., Inoue, N., & Uemura, A. (2018). Pathophysiology of diabetic retinopathy: The old and the new. Diabetes & Metabolism Journal, 42(5), 364–376. https://doi.org/10.4093/dmj.2018.0182
DOI: 10.4093/dmj.2018.0182
Journal: Diabetes & Metabolism Journal
Year: 2018
Publication type: Narrative review
Research domain: Diabetic retinopathy pathophysiology; retinal vascular biology; pharmacological therapeutics (anti-VEGF, corticosteroids, angiopoietin-targeted agents)
2. Study Type Classification
Classification: Narrative/scoping review of DR pathophysiology
This article is a narrative review synthesizing clinical, translational, and basic science evidence on the cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying DR. It does not report original empirical data, perform meta-analysis, or apply systematic review methodology. The article covers clinical management (NPDR, PDR, DME), pharmacological therapies (anti-VEGF, corticosteroids, angiopoietin modulators), and emerging molecular pathways (VEGF signaling, angiopoietin-Tie2 axis, pericyte biology, retinal neovascularization, oxidative stress/inflammation). It briefly references AI-based systems as a future management tool for DR but does not constitute an AI/deep learning study.
3. Research Problem
Problem addressed: The cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying the pathophysiology of DR are not fully determined, largely because hyperglycemic animal models only reproduce limited aspects of subclinical and early DR (p. 364). The review addresses the need for a comprehensive understanding of DR pathophysiology to support development of new pharmacological treatments beyond anti-VEGF agents.
Relation to AI/DL: Peripheral. A single sentence mentions that multimodal retinal imaging data “might be able to be integrated by artificial intelligence-based systems in the future management of DR” (p. 365, citing Schmidt-Erfurth et al., 2018). No AI/deep learning methodology, dataset, or experiment is described.
Primary focus areas: Blood-retina barrier breakdown, retinal neovascularization, VEGF/angiopoietin signaling, pericyte loss, oxidative stress, inflammation, and emerging drug targets.
4. Datasets Used
[NOT APPLICABLE] — This is a narrative review article. No primary datasets, imaging datasets, or deep learning benchmarks are used.
5. Preprocessing Pipeline
[NOT APPLICABLE] — No image preprocessing, augmentation, or computational pipeline is described. The article discusses clinical imaging modalities (OCT, OCTA, ultra-widefield ophthalmoscopy, FA, adaptive optics SLO, ERG) only in a clinical diagnostic context.
6. Model Architecture
[NOT APPLICABLE] — No deep learning model, CNN, Vision Transformer, or any computational architecture is described or evaluated.
7. Validation Design
[NOT APPLICABLE] — As a narrative review, no validation design is employed. The article synthesizes existing literature without systematic search methodology or risk-of-bias assessment.
8. Performance Metrics
[NOT APPLICABLE] — No quantitative performance metrics (AUC, sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, F1, Cohen’s Kappa) are reported. Epidemiological statistics mentioned include: prevalence of any DR among diabetic populations is 34.6% (93 million worldwide); PDR prevalence is 6.96%; DME prevalence is 6.81% (all from Yau et al., 2012, as cited on p. 364).
9. Authors’ Claims
Pathophysiology claims: Vision loss in DR is ascribed primarily to retinal vascular abnormalities—hyperpermeability, hypoperfusion, and neoangiogenesis—that lead to anatomical and functional alterations in retinal neurons and glial cells (p. 364).
Pericyte-related claims: Pericyte dropout from retinal capillary walls is responsible for breakdown of the inner blood-retina barrier (p. 370). Transient pericyte depletion during development results in persistent EC-pericyte dissociation with DR-characteristic vascular lesions (p. 370). However, stripping pericytes from adult retinas is insufficient to induce structural/functional vessel alterations (p. 370).
VEGF claims: VEGFA-VEGFR2 signaling is pivotal in retinal angiogenesis and vascular leakage in DR (p. 369). Anti-VEGF therapies have revolutionized clinical management but have limited efficacy and potential adverse effects (p. 365).
Ang2 claims: Ang2 is upregulated by hypoxia, VEGFA, and hyperglycemia and facilitates angiogenesis, vascular permeability, and inflammation (p. 370). A positive feedback loop between Ang2 and FOXO1 further destabilizes vessel integrity (p. 370).
Neurodegeneration claims: Clinical and experimental evidence demonstrates irreversible loss of neurons preceding vascular lesions in diabetic retinas (p. 369).
Clinical applicability claims: Treatment regimens combining PRP, anti-VEGF agents, and corticosteroids should be optimized depending on individual patient status (p. 367). Novel anti-inflammatory drugs and neuroprotective agents are desired (p. 369).
AI claim: Multimodal imaging data might be integrated by AI-based systems in the future management of DR (p. 365). No further elaboration or evidence is provided.
10. Empirical Support Assessment
As a narrative review, the article does not present original data and therefore no direct empirical support assessment applies. The authors cite 94 references spanning epidemiological studies, randomized controlled trials, basic science experiments, and animal models. The article draws on well-established clinical trial evidence for anti-VEGF efficacy (RISE, RIDE, VIVID-DME, VISTA-DME, CLARITY) and translational mouse model work from the authors’ own laboratory (Ogura et al., 2017; Fukushima et al., 2011). Claims are generally well-sourced but reflect the inherent limitations of narrative reviews: no systematic search strategy, no risk-of-bias assessment, and potential selection bias in cited literature.
11. Internal Validity
Not directly applicable (no original experiment). However, for assessment of the review itself: the article lacks a systematic search methodology, does not use PRISMA or similar reporting standards, and does not formally assess risk of bias or quality of cited studies. The narrative format introduces potential citation selection bias. Several claims are qualified appropriately with hedging language (e.g., “might explain,” “remains unknown,” “is not fully understood”), which is appropriate but limits the strength of some conclusions.
12. External Validity
Cross-population applicability: The review discusses DR globally and cites a large meta-analysis estimating 93 million affected individuals worldwide (Yau et al., 2012). However, most cited clinical trials and experimental models are from Western or East Asian populations; applicability to underrepresented populations is not discussed.
Clinical feasibility: Discussed extensively regarding anti-VEGF drugs, corticosteroids, and emerging therapies (Ang2 blockers, Tie2 activators, neuroprotective agents). Economic burden and repeated injection requirements are noted as practical barriers (p. 367).
Hardware constraints: [NOT REPORTED]
13. Strengths
Comprehensive integration of clinical, translational, and basic science perspectives on DR pathophysiology within a single review. Clear visual schematic (Fig. 2) effectively summarizes the sequential cellular and molecular events in DR progression. Balanced coverage of established (VEGF) and emerging (angiopoietin-Tie2, Sema3E-PlexinD1) therapeutic targets. Transparent acknowledgment of limitations in animal models and current therapies. Excellent coverage of the pericyte biology-to-BRB breakdown pathway with references to the authors’ own primary research. Useful clinical classification table (Table 1) summarizing DR severity, features, and management.
14. Limitations
Explicit (stated by authors)
Hyperglycemic animal models only reproduce limited aspects of subclinical and early DR (p. 364). The molecular backgrounds responsible for retinal vascular abnormalities may vary in individual eyes, as evidenced by differential responses to anti-VEGF drugs and corticosteroids (p. 371). It remains unknown why the retina is preferentially affected in diabetic patients (p. 371). The pathogenic role of outer BRB breakdown in DME is not fully understood (p. 370). Causes and consequences of pericyte dropout in DR require further investigation (p. 370).
Implicit (methodological)
Narrative rather than systematic review methodology—no defined search strategy, inclusion/exclusion criteria, or quality assessment of cited studies. Potential citation selection bias. No quantitative synthesis or meta-analytic pooling. The mention of AI is perfunctory (single sentence) and provides no substantive guidance for computational researchers. No discussion of imaging-based biomarkers or automated grading systems despite referencing advanced imaging modalities. Limited discussion of genetic/genomic factors despite noting genetic susceptibility as a factor in DR initiation.
15. Relevance to My Dissertation
Relevance to preprocessing dominance hypothesis: Minimal. The article does not address image preprocessing, augmentation, or their effects on deep learning performance.
Relevance to cross-database validation: None. No datasets or computational validation frameworks are discussed.
Relevance to EyePACS/Messidor benchmarking: None. No benchmark datasets are mentioned.
Relevance to Vision Transformer comparison: None. No deep learning architectures are discussed.
Potential utility: This article may serve as a domain-knowledge background reference for understanding the clinical and biological basis of DR features (microaneurysms, hemorrhages, exudates, neovascularization) that deep learning models must detect. It provides context for why DR grading scales exist and what pathological features define each severity level. The single-sentence mention of AI integration in DR management (p. 365) could be cited as evidence of the perceived clinical need for automated systems, but the citation is weak and non-substantive.
Risk of contradiction: None. This article does not make claims about AI, preprocessing, or model performance that could contradict dissertation arguments.
16. Citation-Ready Statements
1. Kusuhara et al. (2018) note that DR is the leading cause of vision loss globally in working middle-aged adults, with an estimated prevalence of 34.6% among diabetic populations (p. 364).
2. The authors state that the cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying the pathophysiology of DR are not fully determined, largely because hyperglycemic animal models only reproduce limited aspects of subclinical and early DR (p. 364).
3. Kusuhara et al. (2018) suggest that multimodal retinal imaging data might be integrated by artificial intelligence-based systems in the future management of DR (p. 365).
4. The review identifies pericyte dropout from retinal capillary walls as a consensus mechanism responsible for breakdown of the inner blood-retina barrier in DR (p. 370).
5. Kusuhara et al. (2018) report that clinical and experimental evidence has demonstrated irreversible loss of retinal neurons preceding microvascular changes in diabetic retinas (p. 369).
6. The authors emphasize that the molecular backgrounds responsible for retinal vascular abnormalities may vary across individual eyes with DR, as evidenced by differential responses to anti-VEGF drugs and corticosteroids (p. 371).
17. Epistemic Classification
Classification: Peripheral
Justification: This is a narrative review of DR pathophysiology with no AI/deep learning content, no computational methods, no benchmark datasets, and only a single perfunctory mention of artificial intelligence. While it provides valuable domain knowledge about the biological mechanisms underlying DR features that automated systems must detect, it has no direct methodological relevance to a dissertation focused on deep learning preprocessing, cross-dataset validation, or model architecture comparison for DR grading. It is classified as peripheral rather than foundational because foundational status would require substantive engagement with computational or AI methodology.
18. Analytical Synthesis
Kusuhara et al. (2018) provide a well-structured narrative review of DR pathophysiology that integrates clinical management, VEGF/angiopoietin signaling, pericyte biology, and retinal neovascularization mechanisms. The epistemic weight of this article within a deep learning-focused dissertation is low; it carries no computational methodology, benchmark evaluation, or AI system assessment. Its primary utility lies in providing authoritative biological context for the retinal features (microaneurysms, hemorrhages, exudates, neovascularization, macular edema) that form the target classes in automated DR grading systems. The article’s acknowledgment that DR features vary across individuals and that differential treatment responses exist supports the broader argument that automated systems must be robust to heterogeneous presentations. The single AI reference is too superficial to serve as meaningful support for any dissertation claim about AI integration in DR. This article neither strengthens nor weakens a preprocessing-dominance argument, as it operates in a completely different epistemic domain. It does not demonstrate cross-dataset robustness or address computational generalization. For dissertation positioning, it is best used as a background reference establishing clinical need and pathological feature definitions, cited sparingly and only when biological context is necessary to motivate the computational work.

End of Literature Card
